1: Nebuchadnezzar and all his army fighting against Jerusalem — the siege is the divine instrument of covenant judgment. The gospel's logic follows: Jerusalem under siege is Israel under the weight of covenant failure, which is the human condition apart from Christ. The nations that surround and threaten are the powers of sin, death, and accusation. Christ enters into the full siege of human existence — 'born under the law' (Galatians 4:4) — in order to bear the judgment from within.
2: 'I am going to hand this city over to the king of Babylon, and he will burn it with fire.' The vocabulary of 'handing over' (Hebrew nātan) is the NT's vocabulary of the passion: God 'did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32); Jesus is 'delivered over to death for our trespasses' (Romans 4:25). The city's being handed over to judgment is the type of Christ being handed over to the judgment that should have fallen on the city — on us.
3: Zedekiah will be captured, see the king face to face, and go to Babylon. The face-to-face encounter with the conquering king is the figure of the last judgment — every person will stand before the King (Matthew 25:31-32). But Christ's mediation transforms the encounter: those who are in him will not face condemnation (Romans 8:1) because he faced the full encounter with divine judgment on their behalf. What Zedekiah faced in surrender to Babylon, Christ faced in surrender to the cross.
4: 'Yet hear the word of the LORD, O Zedekiah ... you will not die by the sword.' In the midst of judgment, a word of specific grace. God addresses individuals with particular promises even within general judgment. Jesus's individual address — to the thief on the cross ('today you will be with me in paradise', Luke 23:43), to Zacchaeus ('today salvation has come to this house', Luke 19:9) — is the continuation of this pattern: the word of the LORD reaches specific people with particular assurance even in the hour of judgment.
5: 'You will die in peace.' The paradox: Zedekiah loses his kingdom and his sons but is promised a peaceful death. Christ's peace 'which surpasses all understanding' (Philippians 4:7) is given precisely in the conditions of earthly loss, imprisonment, and apparent defeat. Paul and Silas in prison sing hymns at midnight (Acts 16:25); the peace of God is not contingent on external circumstances because it rests on the finished work of Christ — the true Davidic king who gives the peace that the world cannot give (John 14:27).
6: Jeremiah spoke all these words to Zedekiah — the faithful prophet delivering an unwelcome word to a failing king. Jesus is the faithful and true witness (Revelation 3:14) who speaks truth to power — before Pilate, before the Sanhedrin, in the temple courts — without accommodation. The prophetic pattern of unwelcome truth-telling reaches its climax in Christ, who speaks not only God's word but IS the Word of God, and is rejected and killed for it.
7: Only Lachish and Azekah still hold out — the archaeological record (the Lachish letters) confirms this final-days situation. Historical specificity grounds the theological pattern: the incarnation takes place in specific historical conditions, not in the realm of timeless myth. Luke 2's 'in those days' and John 19's 'it was the day of Preparation for the Passover' insist on the same historicity — the Word became flesh in real time, real geography, real political crisis.
8: Zedekiah made a covenant to proclaim dĕrôr (dĕrôr) — liberty — to all Hebrew slaves. Jesus in the Nazareth synagogue reads Isaiah 61:1-2: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me ... to proclaim liberty (dĕrôr in Hebrew; aphesin in LXX/Luke) to the captives' (Luke 4:18-19). He then says: 'Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.' The Jubilee proclamation of release — which Zedekiah's court enacts and then revokes — is what Christ enacts once and for all and can never revoke: the liberty he proclaims to the captives is permanent (Galatians 5:1: for freedom Christ has set us free).
9: 'Everyone was to release his Hebrew male slave and his Hebrew female slave, so that no one would keep a fellow Hebrew in slavery.' The freedom proclaimed covers the nearest — the fellow Hebrew — but Jesus extends the liberation to all (Galatians 3:28: neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free). The scope of Christ's liberating proclamation transcends ethnic and social boundaries; all who were enslaved to sin are set free by the Son (John 8:34-36: if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed).
10: All the officials and people obeyed — they released their slaves. The initial response to the word is positive; compliance and liberation happen. This mirrors the initial joyful reception of the gospel in many hearts — the seed falls on rocky ground that springs up quickly (Matthew 13:20-21). The test of true liberation is not the initial response but whether the freedom holds under pressure.
11: 'But afterward they turned back and took back the male and female slaves they had freed, forcing them back into slavery.' This is the catastrophic failure of human-willed liberation: when it costs something (the siege temporarily lifted, and the slaves were economically useful), the masters revoked the freedom. The permanence of Christ's liberation is its decisive contrast: 'For sin will have no dominion over you, since you are not under law but under grace' (Romans 6:14). The liberation Christ grants cannot be revoked by human fear or selfishness — it is secured by blood, not by covenant-keeping effort.
12: The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah — divine response to the covenant-breaking. God does not remain silent when his covenant is violated; he speaks with clarity about the consequences. Jesus's warnings in Matthew 23 and 24 follow the same pattern: the rejected prophets, the violated covenant, the coming judgment, spoken with prophetic urgency. The God who speaks through Jeremiah is the same one who speaks in his Son (Hebrews 1:1-2).
13: 'I made a covenant with your ancestors when I brought them out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The Exodus covenant is the basis of the freedom obligation. Paul reads the Exodus through Christ as the new Exodus: 'Our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea, and all were baptized into Moses ... These things happened to them as examples, but they were written down for our instruction' (1 Corinthians 10:1-11). Christ is the true liberator from 'the house of slavery' (Romans 6:17-18: you were slaves of sin but have become obedient from the heart).
14: 'At the end of seven years, every one of you must release a fellow Hebrew.' The sabbatical year liberation foreshadows Christ's Jubilee proclamation (Luke 4:18-19) and the final rest of Hebrews 4. The principle of the seventh year — rest and release after six years of labor — is fulfilled in Christ's completed work: 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is the declaration of the sabbath rest that follows the six days of creation-work. The one who finished the work of redemption is the one who grants the permanent release the sabbatical year only pointed toward.
15: 'Just recently you turned back and did what was right in my eyes, each of you proclaiming liberty to your neighbor. You made a covenant before me in the house that bears my name.' The house bearing God's name is the temple; Jesus identified his body as the true temple (John 2:19-21). The covenant made in the house bearing God's name is now enacted in the body of Christ — the new covenant sealed by his blood in the new temple of his flesh. The liberty proclaimed in the old temple's precincts is fulfilled by the one who is both the temple and the covenant-maker.
16: 'You profaned my name: each of you took back the male and female slaves you had set free.' Profaning the divine name by covenant-breaking is the logic Paul addresses in Romans 6: 'Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? By no means!' (Romans 6:1-2). The freedom of the gospel is not a license to return to slavery — to return to sin after liberation is precisely the profaning of the name that freed us. The permanence of Christ's liberation requires a permanent response of freedom-living.
17: 'You have not obeyed me by proclaiming liberty, each to his brother and to his neighbor. So now I am proclaiming liberty for you — liberty to the sword, to pestilence, and to famine.' The ironic inversion: refuse to grant liberty to others, receive 'liberty' to destruction. Galatians 5:13 contains the counterpart: 'You were called to freedom, brothers. Only do not use your freedom as an opportunity for the flesh, but through love serve one another.' The liberty Christ grants is not a freedom from obligation but a freedom for love-service — using freedom to free others.
18: 'The men who violated my covenant ... when they cut the calf in two and passed between its parts.' The covenant-cutting ceremony of Genesis 15 is recalled: God (as a smoking firepot and flaming torch) passed between the pieces, taking the covenant oath on himself. Hebrews 9:15-22 reads the inauguration of covenants through death: 'Not even the first covenant was inaugurated without blood.' Christ, as the mediator of the new covenant, passes through death itself — not symbolically between the halves of an animal but actually through the valley of death — to ratify the covenant by his own blood.
19: All classes — officials, priests, people — who passed between the calf parts are now subject to the calf's fate: to be cut in two. Covenant violation brings covenant curse. Christ bore the covenant curse for his people: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The curse-logic of the covenant cutting is not abolished but fulfilled — absorbed into the body of the one who passed through death so that we would not have to.
20: 'Their dead bodies will become food for the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.' The final shame of the covenant-breakers — exposed and unburied. The cross appears to be this same shame: Jesus's body exposed on the tree. But the resurrection reverses the shame into glory (Philippians 2:9-11): the body that hung on the cross is now the glorified body at the right hand of God. The resurrection is God's refusal to let the covenant-mediator's body be subject to the dishonor of unburial and corruption (Acts 2:27, 31: God did not abandon him to Hades or let his body see corruption).
21: Zedekiah and his officials given to their enemies — the king who fails to maintain the covenant is himself given over to the very judgment he was supposed to prevent. But in the new covenant, the one who is given over to the enemy (Romans 8:32: he did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all) is not a covenant-breaker but the perfectly faithful covenant-keeper who is given over on behalf of the breakers. The substitution is total: the righteous given over for the unrighteous (1 Peter 3:18).
22: 'I am about to give the command, and I will bring them back to this city ... and burn it with fire. I will make the cities of Judah a desolation without inhabitant.' The finality of judgment on covenant-breaking. But beyond the desolation lies the word of Jeremiah 33: the desolate cities will again hear the voice of joy and gladness (Jeremiah 33:10-11). The gospel's structure is identical: the cross is the desolation, the silencing, the burning — and then the resurrection is the voice that ends the desolation and repopulates the waste places with the new creation.
